text: The lung is retracted posteriorly, exposing the mediastinal pleura and hilum. The surgeon uses a harmonic device for blunt and sharp dissection to divide lymph nodes at station eleven, while displaying the superior pulmonary vein and apical trunk. The black blade of the device vibrates against the white blade, generating thermic energy for simultaneous dissection and coagulation. The superior pulmonary vein is ligated and dissected away using an Ethicon vascular reload, revealing the anatomy of the underlying pulmonary artery.

In the middle of the screen, the apical trunk is dissected free and slinged, while the superior pulmonary vein stump is visible on the left. The apical trunk is divided using an Ethicon stapler with vascular reload after lymph node dissection. The superior vena cava is retracted slightly to allow passage of the stapler, which divides it. The surgeon then moves to the bronchus, where a lymph node at the bifurcation of the secondary carinae serves as a landmark for dissecting around the right upper lobe bronchus. The bronchus is dissected free and divided with a green reload stapler. The harmonic device is used as a blunt instrument over the bronchus to create a free wall for stapler passage. The small recurrent artery is divided with another vascular staple.

The surgeon uses an energy device, such as the harmonic, to seal and divide small vessels up to seven millimeters in diameter. The anatomy of the pulmonary artery is clearly visible, confirming no additional branches to the upper lobe. The AE-VI supplies blood to the apical segment of the lower lobe. Further posterior mediastinal dissection is performed, and the specimen is placed in a bag for removal. The procedure progresses to the lymphadenectomy of station IV-R.

The surgeon opens the pleura over the azygos vein using the harmonic device, which seals small lymphatic vessels and provides hemostatic results. The lymph node grasper retracts the specimen to the left, while the harmonic performs dissection on the right of the screen. The trachea becomes visible posteriorly, with the azygos vein closing the space at the bottom of the screen, the superior vena cava anteriorly, and the arch of the aorta deep medial in the anatomical space.

The surgeon retracts the lung upwards to divide the pulmonary ligament and harvest lymph nodes from station nine. The ligament is retracted superiorly, and station ten lymph nodes over the inferior pulmonary vein are harvested. The lung is then retracted anteriorly, completing the posterior mediastinal dissection upwards over the superior margin of the inferior pulmonary vein. Below the carina, the subcarinal region is dissected, and station seven lymph nodes are harvested. The esophagus is retracted with a sucker, and the harmonic device completes hemostasis after lymph node harvesting, leaving the subcarinal region emptied.
